statement: The most common meter in English poetry is iambic pentameter — ten syllables per line alternating unstressed and stressed beats, used by Shakespeare in his plays and sonnets.
quizQuestion: Which poetic meter did Shakespeare use in his plays and sonnets?
explanation: Iambic pentameter consists of five iambs per line — each iamb being one unstressed syllable followed by one stressed syllable (da-DUM). Ten syllables total per line. Shakespeare used it throughout his plays and 154 sonnets. The pattern mirrors natural English speech rhythms, which is why poets and playwrights adopted it so widely. 'Shall I comPARE thee TO a SUMmer's DAY?' is a perfect example.
wowFactor: Iambic pentameter sounds natural because it matches the human heartbeat — da-DUM, da-DUM, da-DUM — which is why Shakespeare chose it for characters expressing strong emotion.

statement: The haiku is a Japanese poem of exactly 17 syllables in three lines (5-7-5), traditionally centred on a single moment in nature and including a seasonal reference called a kigo.
quizQuestion: What is the seasonal reference word in a traditional Japanese haiku called?
explanation: A kigo is a word or phrase associated with a particular season that is required in traditional haiku. Master Matsuo Bashō (1644–1694) established many conventions, including the kigo. Kireji (cutting word) is another required element — a pause or break that divides the haiku into two juxtaposed images. Bashō's most famous haiku: 'Old pond — / a frog jumps in / sound of water.'
wowFactor: Every traditional haiku must contain a hidden seasonal clock — a single 'kigo' word that tells the reader the time of year without stating it directly.

statement: Ancient Greek and Roman bronze sculptures were made using the lost-wax casting technique, where a wax model is coated in clay, heated to melt out the wax, then molten bronze is poured in.
quizQuestion: Which ancient technique for casting bronze sculptures involves melting out a wax model?
explanation: Lost-wax casting (cire perdue) dates to at least 3500 BCE and was the primary method for making large bronze sculptures in ancient Greece and Rome. A wax model (or wax-coated clay core) is encased in a ceramic shell, heated in a kiln so the wax melts out, and molten bronze is poured into the cavity. After cooling, the ceramic shell is broken away. The original wax is 'lost,' making each casting unique.
wowFactor: Every ancient Greek bronze statue required destroying the original wax sculpture — there could only ever be one bronze. This is why ancient bronze originals are so rare.

It is 11.1 cm tall, carved from oolitic limestone, and painted with red ochre. Its exaggerated female features (prominent breasts, hips, and belly) have led most scholars to interpret it as a fertility figure or goddess idol. It is one of the world's earliest known examples of figurative art.

statement: The Statue of Liberty was originally a shiny copper-red color when unveiled in 1886, and turned its iconic green over 20–30 years through natural oxidation forming copper carbonate patina.
quizQuestion: What was the Statue of Liberty's original color when unveiled in 1886?
explanation: The statue's outer skin is made of 3mm-thick copper sheets — about the thickness of two US pennies. When unveiled in 1886 it gleamed like a giant copper penny. Atmospheric oxidation gradually converted the surface to copper carbonate (verdigris), turning it green. The full patina took about 20–30 years to develop completely, alarming early observers who thought the statue was rotting.
wowFactor: The Statue of Liberty's green color is literally the statue rusting — the same chemical process that turns copper pipes green turned the most famous sculpture in America into a giant green monument.
